summary: Azure Key Vault is a managed cloud service for securely storing and controlling access to secrets, encryption keys, and TLS/SSL certificates. It offers two tiers: Standard (software-based FIPS 140 Level 1) and Premium (HSM-backed FIPS 140-3 Level 3). It integrates with Microsoft Entra ID for authentication and supports Azure RBAC or Key Vault access policies for authorization.

key_concepts.explanation: Securely stores tokens, passwords, API keys, and connection strings so applications retrieve them via URI rather than hardcoding them.
Creates and controls encryption keys used to encrypt data, with optional HSM protection in the Premium tier.
Provisions, manages, and auto-renews public/private TLS/SSL certificates for Azure and internal resources.
Standard uses software-based FIPS 140 Level 1 encryption; Premium uses hardware HSMs validated to FIPS 140-3 Level 3 for the highest cryptographic protection.
Authentication via Microsoft Entra ID; authorization via Azure RBAC (management and data plane) or Key Vault access policies (data plane only).
Key Vault access logs can be archived to a storage account, streamed to an Event Hub, or sent to Azure Monitor Logs.
Key Vault contents are replicated within a region and to a secondary region automatically, with no admin action needed for failover.

exam_focus_points: Difference between Azure RBAC and Key Vault access policies: RBAC covers both management and data plane; access policies cover data plane only
Standard tier uses FIPS 140 Level 1 (software); Premium tier uses FIPS 140-3 Level 3 (HSM-backed)
Authentication to Key Vault is via Microsoft Entra ID
Key Vault logging destinations: Storage Account, Event Hub, Azure Monitor Logs
Key Vault integrates with Azure Disk Encryption, Azure SQL TDE/Always Encrypted, and Azure App Service
Secrets are accessed via URI, including specific version retrieval
Key Vault replicates data to a secondary region for HA without admin intervention
similar_concepts: Microsoft Entra ID (identity vs. secret management confusion)
common_mistakes: Confusing Key Vault access policies with Azure RBAC — policies only control data plane access, not management plane
Thinking Standard tier uses HSMs — it uses software-based cryptography; only Premium tier has HSM-protected keys
Assuming Microsoft can access your keys — Key Vault is designed so Microsoft cannot see or extract stored data
Forgetting that each application should have its own Key Vault for proper secret segregation
Mixing up Key Vault (secrets/keys/certs store) with Azure Managed HSM (dedicated single-tenant HSM service)
